skill.name: quality-gates
skill.description: Run quality gates for code projects, including linting, type checks, tests, builds, dependency audits, and pre-commit checks.
skill.summary: Run structured quality checks before commits, pushes, releases, or deployments.
skill.author: AI-Vibe-Prompts

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many command-execution patterns, plus sensitive-file, hidden-file, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance indicators. Review found no malicious intent or prompt injection, but confirmed that the skill intentionally runs repository commands and can modify files, install packages, contact package registries, and remove build artifacts. Publish with a warning that users should run it only in trusted repositories and review commands before execution.

medium_findings.title: Repository Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run npm, npx, test, build, audit, git, find, grep, xargs, and shell commands in the target repository. This is expected for quality gates, but project-defined scripts and package binaries may execute arbitrary code in an untrusted repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and numerous, and they include project-controlled npm scripts and npx package execution. The intent is legitimate quality validation, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Automated File Modification and Cleanup
medium_findings.description: The workflow suggests auto-fix commands and cleanup commands such as lint fixes, audit fixes, and removal of build output directories. These operations can change working tree state or delete generated artifacts if executed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced commands are directly documented and can modify files or remove directories. The commands target normal quality workflows, but they still require user review in shared repositories.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Static Findings Are Defensive Checks
low_findings.description: The lines flagged for environment file access are instructions to detect staged secrets and staged .env filenames before commit. They do not instruct the agent to read or exfiltrate environment files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text says the purpose is to prevent sensitive data leaks. The commands inspect staged diffs and names rather than loading secrets for transmission.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto, Hidden File, and Reconnaissance Flags Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography flags point to descriptive or commit-format text, the hidden-file flag points to changed TypeScript file extensions, and the reconnaissance flag points to another skill name. No evidence found of cryptographic use, hidden file harvesting, or network scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found these detections are caused by benign Markdown text and examples. There is no semantic evidence that these lines perform the flagged behaviors.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project-Controlled Package Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands such as npm run lint, npm run typecheck, npm run test, and npm run build execute scripts defined by the target project. In untrusted repositories, these scripts may perform arbitrary actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly tells agents to execute project-defined npm scripts. This is a known trust boundary even though it is consistent with the skill purpose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Network and Modification Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands such as npm audit, pnpm audit, npm audit fix, npm install, and npx husky init can contact package registries or modify dependency and hook files. Users should approve these steps before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package-manager commands are explicit and have normal network or file-modification side effects. The skill does not hide them, so the concern is user consent and repository trust.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Pipeline and Deletion Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes shell pipelines and an rm -rf cleanup example. These commands are common in developer workflows, but they can cause damage if adapted incorrectly or run in the wrong directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are visible in the skill and include deletion plus xargs pipelines. They are narrowly aimed at build artifacts and local analysis, so risk is moderate.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent checks before commits, pull requests, and deployments. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through linting, type checks, tests, builds, audits, and release readiness checks.
actual_capabilities: Detects common JavaScript and TypeScript quality tools from project files.
Runs linting, type checking, tests, builds, and dependency audits in a staged workflow.
Checks staged changes for secrets, .env files, oversized files, and commit quality issues.
Supports progressive gate levels for pre-commit, pre-push, and pre-deploy validation.
Reports pass, fail, skipped status, durations, errors, coverage, and recommended fixes.
Suggests Husky and lint-staged integration for repeatable local checks.
limitations: It focuses on JavaScript and TypeScript projects and may need adaptation for other stacks.
It depends on the project scripts and tools already installed in the repository.
It cannot prove production readiness beyond the checks that are configured and executed.
Some commands may modify files, install packages, or contact package registries.

use_cases.title: Validate a Feature Branch
use_cases.description: Run linting, type checks, unit tests, and build verification before opening a pull request.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Release Candidate
use_cases.description: Apply the full gate sequence, including dependency audit and build checks, before deployment approval.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Checks
use_cases.description: Document repeatable quality gates and optional Git hook setup for a shared repository.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic Gates
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner validation before committing small changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the essential quality gates for this repository. Check linting and type checking, then summarize pass or fail results.

prompt_templates.title: Check Pull Request Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard validation before opening a pull request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run quality gates for my current branch. Include linting, type checks, tests, build verification, and any staged secret checks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Release Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full pre-deploy quality gate workflow. Include tests, coverage, dependency audit, build verification, and clear release blockers.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Project Gate Policy
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup for a team workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this repository and propose a quality gate policy for pre-commit, pre-push, and pre-deploy checks. Include commands, thresholds, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: Run quality gates before I commit this feature.
output: Type checking failed with three errors.
Tests and build were skipped because the type gate failed.
Next action: fix the reported type errors and rerun the gates.

output_examples.input: Check whether this branch is ready for release.
output: Release gate summary: most checks passed.
Unit tests passed and coverage exceeded the configured threshold.
Dependency audit found one high vulnerability.
Release is blocked until the dependency issue is fixed or formally accepted.

output_examples.input: Set up repeatable local quality checks for this repo.
output: Recommended workflow: run linting and type checks before commit.
Run tests and dependency audit before push.
Run build, integration tests, and security audit before deployment.
Review hook installation commands before applying changes.

best_practices: Review commands before execution, especially package scripts, installs, audit fixes, and cleanup commands.
Start with fast gates during development and reserve full release gates for push or deploy workflows.
Keep thresholds in project configuration so results are consistent across local and CI environments.
anti_patterns: Running project-defined scripts from an untrusted repository without inspecting them first.
Using bypass flags such as no-verify as a routine way to skip failed checks.
Treating warnings, skipped gates, or missing test coverage as release approval.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: It provides workflows that use shell and package-manager commands. Users should review command execution in untrusted repositories.

faq.question: Which project types work best?
faq.answer: It works best for JavaScript and TypeScript projects with npm, pnpm, ESLint, Biome, Jest, Vitest, or similar tooling.

faq.question: Can it fix problems automatically?
faq.answer: It can suggest lint fixes, format fixes, and audit fixes. Some fixes may change files or dependencies.

faq.question: Does it replace CI?
faq.answer: No. It helps run local and agent-assisted checks, but CI should remain the source of truth for shared branches.

faq.question: How should teams use it before releases?
faq.answer: Run the comprehensive gate level and treat failed tests, failed builds, and high security issues as release blockers.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate quality-check intent, but it executes repository commands and may modify files or contact package registries.
